mnemonics.title: Negation of Quantifiers
mnemonics.content: 'All' becomes 'Some not' (∃), 'Some' becomes 'None' (∀). Think: Flip the quantifier and negate the predicate. NOT ALL = AT LEAST ONE NOT; NONE = ALL NOT.

mnemonics.title: Order Matters for Nested Quantifiers
mnemonics.content: When quantifiers are different (one ∀, one ∃), swap changes meaning: 'Everyone loves someone' (different person for each) ≠ 'Someone loves everyone' (same person). When both same (∀∀ or ∃∃), order doesn't matter.

mnemonics.title: Universal vs Existential Proofs
mnemonics.content: UNIVERSAL: Pick arbitrary, prove, generalize (like proving property holds for ALL by showing it holds for any arbitrary member). EXISTENTIAL: Find witness, construct example, show existence.

mnemonics.title: Predicate vs Proposition
mnemonics.content: PREDICATE has free variables (open sentence), PROPOSITION has no free variables (all bound by quantifiers or assigned values). PREDICATE: x > 5 (ask 'which x?'). PROPOSITION: ∃x, x > 5 (true).

acronyms.usage: Used to make statements about every element in domain: ∀x P(x)
Used to assert existence of at least one element: ∃x P(x)
A statement about x that becomes proposition when x is bound or value assigned
Proof technique: prove for arbitrary a, then conclude ∀x P(x)
Proof technique: assume antecedent, prove consequent

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a big box containing ALL elements of domain. Statement ∀x P(x) means the property P is TRUE for EVERY item in the box without exception.
Imagine searching through domain. Statement ∃x P(x) means you find AT LEAST ONE item with property P (could be one or more).
Think of a 'barrier' with quantifier. To negate, push NOT through the barrier: NOT (∀) becomes ∃, NOT (∃) becomes ∀. The predicate gets NOT too.
Draw a 2D table with rows for x and columns for y. ∀x∃y: for each row, find SOME column with checkmark. ∃y∀x: find ONE column that has checkmark in EVERY row.
Think of quantifier as 'binder' that grabs the variable. Bound variable is captured (belongs to quantifier). Free variable is outside, can 'run away' - not fixed.

analogies.analogy: Like a quality check where EVERY item on production line must pass test. No item is exempt.
Like finding ONE friend who can help you. You only need at least one, not all.
Statement 'All fruits are sweet' is false if you find ONE fruit that is NOT sweet. One counterexample defeats 'all'.
Statement 'Some students passed' is false if NO students passed - you need ZERO to make it false.
'Every person has a mother' (∀person ∃mother) is true: each person has SOME mother (may be different). 'Someone is mother of everyone' (∃mother ∀person) is false.
Like proving a mathematical formula works for ALL numbers by picking a 'representative' number and showing it works - your representative stands for all.
